title: Tanzania Ward Matching, Causal Debiasing & Spatial Policy Analysis

yaml.title: Tanzania Ward Matching, Causal Debiasing & Spatial Policy Analysis
yaml.description: Geospatial fuzzy matching of 3,944 Tanzanian administrative wards across a boundary change, Monte Carlo proof of collider bias, and choropleth mapping of U.S. animal welfare enforcement.

markdown.headingText: Part 1: Geospatial fuzzy matching — Tanzania ward redistricting (2010–2015)
markdown.markdown:
```{=html}
Fuzzy matching ArcGIS · spmap Causal inference Choropleth mapping
Course
PPOL 6818 · Georgetown University
Data scale
3,944 wards · 1,200 simulations
Tools
Stata, ArcGIS, spmap, shp2dta
GitHub
View code ↗
``` Tanzania's ward count grew from **3,333 to 3,944** between 2010 and 2015 through administrative subdivision. Matching electoral datasets across this boundary change required reconciling two non-overlapping geographic classifications — a standard problem in any longitudinal sub-national policy analysis. **Method:** Used ArcGIS shapefile intersection data (2012 and 2017 shapefiles) to compute the percentage area overlap between each 2015 ward and all candidate 2010 parent wards. Applied a 50% area-overlap threshold to classify parent-child relationships. ```{=html}
2,705
Stable wards (unchanged 2010–2015)
488
Split into exactly 2 new wards
45
Split into 3 or more wards
440 / 616
Parentless / orphan wards
``` **Top regions by ward division rate:** | Region | Division rate | Context | |---|---|---| | Rukwa | 39.7% | Highest — major administrative restructuring | | Morogoro | 34.7% | | | Katavi | 28.6% | | | Mtwara | 28.5% | | | Arusha | 25.6% | | | Kagera | 5.0% | Lowest — minimal redistricting | **Policy relevance:** This kind of boundary reconciliation is essential for any longitudinal analysis of sub-national government data — elections, health outcomes, census linkages, or fiscal transfers. ## Part 2: Debiasing treatment effect estimates — Monte Carlo simulation Designed a DGP with a **known treatment effect (β = 0.3 SD)**, then built 5 regression models varying which controls were included — confounder, mediator, and collider — running 200 simulations at each of 6 sample sizes. **Key finding:** Only Model 2 (controlling for the confounder alone) recovers β = 0.30. A larger sample size *cannot fix structural bias* — Models 1 and 5 remain wrong at N=2,500. | Model | Controls included | Bias direction | Recovers β = 0.30? | |---|---|---|---| | M1: Naive | None | Upward (~0.57) | ❌ | | **M2: Correct** | **Confounder only** | **None** | **✅** | | M3: + Mediator | Confounder + mediator | None (higher variance) | ✅ | | M4: Collider | Confounder + collider | Downward (~0.15) | ❌ | | M5: All | All covariates | Fully attenuated (~0.04) | ❌ | **Collider warning:** Including a variable caused by both treatment and outcome introduces downward bias of ~0.15 — a counterintuitive and commonly overlooked error in applied policy research that *gets worse* with more data. ## Part 3: Spatial analysis — U.S. animal welfare enforcement choropleth Merged a hand-coded **Animal Welfare Penalty Harshness Index** (0–100 scale, 48 states) with U.S. Census shapefiles in Stata using `spmap`, producing a choropleth revealing stark regional disparities in enforcement. **Method:** `shp2dta` to import Census shapefiles → merge with penalty index → `spmap` with quantile color breaks → explicit handling of missing data (WY, SD) shown in legend as gray. **Finding:** Northeast and Pacific Coast states score 75+; the Deep South and Mountain West cluster below 35 — consistent with broader regulatory divergence patterns in U.S. federalism. ```{=html}
Skills demonstrated
Geospatial fuzzy matching Shapefile processing Panel data reconciliation Stata bysort / merge DAG reasoning Collider / confounder identification Choropleth mapping ArcGIS · spmap
```
